Latest News
  1. [![Lakeview lane vertical 2-26-26 copy](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=69907)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3188)
####  [Heads up in east Anaheim: Plan for 91 Freeway ramp and lane closures ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3188)
Heads up in east Anaheim: Plan for 91 Freeway ramp and lane closures at Lakeview Avenue starting 9 p.m. on Friday, Feb. 27, through 4 a.m. Monday, March 2. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3188)
  2. [![The Anaheim Resort](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3167)
####  [Anaheim approves hotelier plan to fund affordable housing for workers ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3167)
Hotels around Anaheim’s theme parks and convention center will fund affordable housing for hospitality workers with final action Tuesday at Anaheim’s City Council meeting. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3167)
  3. [![city news graphic](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2731)
####  [Latest: updated hearing date for Ament motion to withdraw guilty plea; running timeline ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2731)
Anaheim has condemned actions documented in a federal investigation of the city's former mayor and a former Anaheim Chamber of Commerce executive that came to light in 2022. Here is what has happened, how Anaheim has responded and ongoing updates. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2731)
  4. [![Image to go with a press release for Anaheim Contigo and information on immigration enforcement](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3137)
####  [Anaheim Contigo: go to for timely immigration enforcement information ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3137)
Anaheim Contigo is your source for timely information about federal immigration enforcement activity in our city. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3137)
  5. [![20250514-DSC00828](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3086)
####  [New state law covers space near crosswalks for safer streets ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3086)
A new California law limits how close you can park near crosswalks in Anaheim neighborhoods and across the state. The state law aims to improve visibility and make streets safer for kids, families and everyone who walks, bikes or drives. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=3086)
  6. [![center greens ](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2996)
####  [Center Greens Park debuts downtown ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2996)
Center Greens Park debuted downtown Saturday, transforming a grassy, informal space into an iconic park with a new skate park, walking paths, butterfly garden and more for community to enjoy. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2996)
  7. [![news flash La Palma groundbreaking](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2984)
####  [La Palma Park Way Promenade Groundbreaking ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2984)
Steeped in history, La Palma Park promenade project starts multiyear transformation of iconic park [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2984)
  8. [![two volunteers building a rock climbing wall for new playground at little people's park ](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2924)
####  [Iconic downtown Anaheim park gets new kid-designed, volunteer-built, donated playground ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2924)
One of Anaheim’s most iconic parks got a new playground designed by neighborhood kids and built in a day by more than 100 volunteers from The CarMax Foundation, KABOOM!, Anaheim Family YMCA and the city of Anaheim. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2924)
  9. [![city news graphic](https://www.anaheim.net/)](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2847)
####  [Anaheim shares state audit, supports recommendations with adoption underway ](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2847)
Anaheim has shared a state audit critical of contract and funding oversight involving Visit Anaheim and the Anaheim Chamber of Commerce and welcomes the report’s recommendations with adoption already underway. [Read on...](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?AID=2847)


[View All News](https://www.anaheim.net/CivicAlerts.aspx?CID=58)

#  Specific Plans 
[![Specific Plans Map 01172019](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=24777)](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/2456/Specific-Plans-Map-)
The City of Anaheim has approved 11 specific plans which provide focused guidance and regulation in designated areas. Each specific plan includes land use, circulation and infrastructure plans along with zoning requirements and design guidelines. View our [map of the specific plans](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/2456) in the City of Anaheim.  

Specific Plan | Zoning Code  
---|---  
[](https://www.anaheim.net/1061/Highlands)[The Highlands at Anaheim Hills](https://www.anaheim.net/1061/Highlands) (SP87-1)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18100highlandsatanaheimhillsspeci?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.100)[Chapter 18.100](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-71786)  
[](https://www.anaheim.net/1059/Sycamore-Canyon)[](https://www.anaheim.net/1059/Sycamore-Canyon)[Sycamore Canyon](https://www.anaheim.net/1059/Sycamore-Canyon) (SP88-1)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18102sycamorecanyonspecificplanno?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.102)[Chapter 18.102](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-71943)  
[The Summit of Anaheim Hills ](https://www.anaheim.net/1058/Summit-of-Anaheim-Hills)(SP88-2)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18104thesummitofanaheimhillsspeci?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.104)[Chapter 18.104](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-72093)  
[PacifiCenter](https://www.anaheim.net/1052/PacifiCenter) (SP88-3 Rescinded 1/26/16)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18106pacificenteranaheimspecificp?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.106)Chapter 18.106  
[The Anaheim Hills Festival](https://www.anaheim.net/1060/Anaheim-Hills-Festival) (SP90-1)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18108festivalspecificplanno90-1sp?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.108)[Chapter 18.108](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-72350)  
[East Center Street](https://www.anaheim.net/1037/East-Center-Street) (SP90-2)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18110eastcenterstreetdevelopments?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.110)[Chapter 18.110](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-72578)  
[Mountain Park ](https://www.anaheim.net/1040/Mountain-Park)(SP90-4)  
|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18112mountainparkspecificplanno90?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.112)[Chapter 18.112](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-72750)

|  [](http://www.amlegal.com/nxt/gateway.dll/California/anaheim/title18zoning/chapter18114disneylandresortspecificplan?f=templates$fn=default.htm$3.0$vid=amlegal:anaheim_ca$anc=JD_Chapter18.114)[Chapter 18.114](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-73174)

#  About the Utilities 
## Overview
  * Largest population in Orange County
  * 10th largest population in California
  * Area: 50 square miles
  * Average annual rainfall: 13.4 inches
  * Average annual temperature: 67 degrees
  * Annual visitors: more than 25 million
  * Population: 360,000


Anaheim residents are more than utilities customers, they are owners of their utilities. They provide input to the decision process both directly and through an appointed resident advisory Public Utilities Board. With final authority vested in Anaheim's elected City Council, decisions are made in the best interest of our residents, quality of life, and local economy. As a municipal, not-for-profit utilities, our rates are based on our costs of providing water and electricity. 
## Local Ownership, Local Control
  

Anaheim Public Utilities is a city-owned, not-for-profit electric and water utility that offers quality electric and water services to residents and businesses in Anaheim at rates among the lowest in Orange County.
[![Electric Services](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=31367)](https://www.anaheim.net/2104/)
[![Water Services](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=31366)](https://www.anaheim.net/1694/)
[![Who governs the Utilities](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=31219)](https://www.anaheim.net/1930/)
## Anaheim has been hard at work serving its residents ever since its foundation in 1857
  
For more than 150 years, the city has cultivated the ability to rise to any challenge with innovative solutions to better the community. In 1879, Anaheim’s leaders took responsibility for operating the water system for their fledgling city. Then, in 1894, they seized the opportunity to build the first municipal electric utility in Southern California, changing the face of the region for years to come. 
Such reliable, affordable water and power attracted businesses and families, evolving Anaheim into a leading industrial and manufacturing center. In the 1950s, the city’s many unique qualities attracted the attention of the famous Walt Disney, who was looking for the perfect place to build his dream park. Anaheim boldly embraced the opportunity, and was soon catapulted onto the world stage - first as a dynamic tourist destination and later as a bustling convention, sports and entertainment hub. 
As a result, the Anaheim of today looks much different from the Anaheim of the past — and its demands for water and electricity are far higher than our ancestors could ever have imagined. But the dedication of the city to serving its community and meeting challenges head-on has remained unchanged. Our residential electric rates are the lowest in Orange County, and we still work hard to deliver quality water at a competitive price. We also offer a value-packed array of [rebates and incentives](https://www.anaheim.net/404/Rebates-Incentives) — helping our customers make more efficient and economical use of the critical resources we provide.
[![Public Utilities Home Page](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=42943)](https://www.anaheim.net/6099)

  6. Turf Replacement Program


#  Turf Replacement Program 
![Ditch the Turf banner](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=65290)
Anaheim and its regional partners are encouraging residents and businesses to help the environment by reducing potable water use for the irrigation of lawns.
One option for customers is to ditch their grass turf by planting native, drought-tolerant plants that**promote biodiversity** , significantly **reduce water use** by using drip irrigation (by as much as 70%), and**reduce heat islanding** when low water-use trees and plants are installed rather than pavers or synthetic turf.
Replace turf (grass) with water-efficient landscaping and get more back!
### Enhanced Turf Replacement Program Rebate
Through the Metropolitan Water District of Southern California’s (Metropolitan) SoCal Water$mart Program, Anaheim Public Utilities customers are eligible to receive $3 per square foot of turf removed, up to 5,000 sq. ft. for residents.
**Combine with our TreePower program** for [4 free trees](https://www.anaheim.net/FormCenter/Public-Utilities-6/TreePower-Program-58) to support your project! Choose from more than 40 available species! Select “Trees for Turf Removal” when you apply.
**Contact us for a free consultation** to discuss turf replacement options and rebate requirements*.  
Call 714-765-4218. 
* * *
_*Rebates are available on a first-come, first-served basis. Synthetic turf is not eligible._
  

### Turf Replacement Program Rebate Requirements
  
To be eligible for a rebate, you **must apply and receive project start approval before removing any turf.**
**Synthetic turf is not eligible.  
**
Please visit [socalwatersmart.com](https://www.socalwatersmart.com/) to view program requirements and begin the application process.  
  
New front yard and/or parkway projects must follow [City of Anaheim Landscaping Requirements](https://anaheim.net/1064/City-of-Anaheim-Landscaping-Requirements). Prior to installation, the **Anaheim Planning Department must review and approve the proposed landscape design.** Please call Planning Services at 714-765-5139 for assistance.
###

  5. Volunteer & Civic Engagement


#  Volunteer & Civic Engagement 
The City of Anaheim has a variety of volunteer opportunities to suit your needs, from volunteering with the Fire Department, the Police Department, or with the Community Services Department.
To learn more about volunteer opportunities throughout Anaheim, we encourage you to visit the following resources:
  * City of Anaheim Volunteer Opportunities
  * Community Groups
  * Fire Department Volunteer Opportunities
  * Police Department Volunteer Opportunities
  * Volunteer Center of Orange County
  * Volunteer Match


* * *
![Anaheim Volunteer Recognition](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=42674)
Join us for our annual celebration recognizing the City of Anaheim’s Volunteers, Business Sponsors, Service Organizations, and Individual Donors.

  7. Renting the Amphitheatre


#  Renting the Amphitheatre 
![Anaheim Concert Series4](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=40369)
Pearson Park Amphitheatre is the perfect blend of Spanish architecture and modern-age technology. The 2,000-seat outdoor venue is enclosed with screened gates and surrounded by beautiful bougainvillea and wisteria-covered arbors.
## Rental Form
  

Please complete this online form to start your rental request of the Pearson Park Amphitheatre. 
  * [Pearson Park Amphitheatre Online Rental Form](https://www.anaheim.net/FormCenter/Community-Services-4/Pearson-Park-Amphitheatre-Theatre-Rental-159)


## Accessibility
  

Easily accessible from the Santa Ana (5), Orange (57) and Riverside (91) freeways, the amphitheatre is fit to accommodate event producers, non-profit organizations, residents, and corporations who need the perfect place for concerts, festivals, services, theatre productions, weddings and more.
##    
Availability & Features
  

Available for rent during the months of April through October, facility rental includes the intimate courtyard, complete with sheltered arbors and a centerpiece fountain. Our backstage area includes dressing rooms with a large vanity, mirrors, restrooms and air conditioning! Tables and chairs are also available upon request.
  

Our friendly, knowledgeable and helpful staff will be with you throughout your entire event to provide you with exceptional service.

#  Neighborhood Services 
![Helping Neighborhoods Help Themselves](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=14164)
## Mission
To lead a city-wide initiative aimed at enhancing the quality of life in Anaheim neighborhoods. We empower residents to take charge of their communities by facilitating collaboration between the city and various community resources. Together, we strive to create safe, vibrant, and thriving neighborhoods where every resident can thrive.
### About Us
Neighborhood Services is the dedicated resource for residents seeking assistance and support. Unsure of which city department to contact? Have questions, ideas, or concerns about your neighborhood? Look no further – we're here to help! At Neighborhood Services, we believe in fostering partnerships between residents, stakeholders, and city resources to address neighborhood concerns and drive positive change.
### Council Districts
Anaheim operates under a "By-District" system for electing City Council members, consisting of six electoral districts, with the Mayor elected at large. Understanding your district is key to engaging with local governance and making your voice heard in shaping the future of Anaheim.
###   

### **Not sure which district you reside in?**
### **Explore our interactive map below to find out!**
[![Neighborhood District Map 2017](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=23093)](https://gis.anaheim.net/portal/apps/instant/lookup/index.html?appid=1fb012fbdc094352acfdce4c6015dfb7 "Neighborhood District Map 2017")

#  Permits & Requirements 
The City of Anaheim has over 60 spacious parks where you can get active and enjoy the outdoors. Our facility-staff is here to assist you in obtaining an athletic field, special event, or open space permit. 
## Applications
  * [Picnic Shelter Application](http://anaheim.net/play)
    * See below for the 2026 Picnic Shelter and Open Area fee schedule. Please note that not all shelter facilities offer sink and/or electrical access. Visit anaheim.net/play for more detailed facility information. Reservations must be made _at minimum_ 3 days in advance.
  * [Open Area Application](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68055/2026-Open-Area-Application)
    * Open Area applications are to be submitted for gatherings of 25 or more in parks that do not offer Picnic Shelter facilities. Please note that gatherings over 100 are considered Special Events.
  * [Special Event Application](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/37724/Special-Event-Application-_PDF)
    * All Special Event Applications are required _at least_ 180 days prior to the event and will be accepted up to one year in advance of the event. Please be prepared to provide a proposed event layout.
  * [Athletic Field Application](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68028/2026-Application-Packet-FINAL)
    * A valid Certificate of Insurance compliant with the requirements outlined in the Athletic Field Application Packet will be required prior to permit allocation. Applications must be received _at least_ two weeks prior to the requested reservation date. All applications received within 10 days of the reservation date will be assessed a **$28 permit rush fee.**
  * [Film Permit Application](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68005/Film-Permit-Application)
    * All filming conducted in a City Park must first be approved by the Community Services Facility Scheduling Section. Please submit your application to facilityscheduling@anaheim.net or contact (714) 765-5155 for more information.


## Regulations & Requirements
All use of City of Anaheim parks and Community Services facilities are governed by the policies and procedures described in our policy manual and Chapter 13.08 of the Anaheim Municipal Code. 
[**Parks, Fields, Facilities and Open Space Use Policies and Procedures**](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68027/2026-Facilities-Policy-and-Procedure-Manual-FINAL)
[**Anaheim Municipal Code, Chapter 13.08 Public Parks**](https://codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/anaheim/latest/anaheim_ca/0-0-0-60982)
![Park Rules Sign](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=68034)
## Additional Resources
  * [2026 Athletic Field Fees](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68009/2026-Athletic-Field-Fees)
  * [2026 Picnic Shelter Fees](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68029/2026-Picnic-Shelter-Open-Area-Fees)
  * [Picnic Shelter FAQs](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68007/Picnic-Shelter-FAQ-Flyer)
  * [Picnic Shelter Reservation Instructions](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/68008/How-to-Reserve-a-Picnic-Shelter)


## Contact Us
Facility Scheduling Office (714) 765-5276 Fax: (714) 765-4454 facilityscheduling@anaheim.net Monday-Friday, 8:00 AM - 5:00 PM

#  City Administration 
## Anaheim city manager
  
Anaheim's city manager is responsible for the daily operations of one of California’s largest cities with a $2 billion annual budget, nearly 350,000 residents, 20,000 businesses and 25 million yearly visitors.
The city manager works with Anaheim’s mayor and City Council to implement their policy direction for the city.
Anaheim Assistant City Manager Greg Garcia is currently acting city manager.
The city manager oversees 11 city departments, including Anaheim Public Utilities, the Anaheim Police Department, Anaheim Fire & Rescue and Convention, Sports & Entertainment, operator of the Anaheim Convention Center.
## City Administration Calendars

  4. Communications 


#  Communications 
The Communications office informs residents, businesses and visitors about the city of Anaheim through media relations, publications, social media and video operations. The Communications office ensures Anaheim residents stay up to date with city news regarding programs, services, policy actions and events. In addition, the office encourages residents to use a variety of communication tools that connect residents with the city of Anaheim easily 24 hours a day, seven days a week from any location.

  5. [Water & Energy Saving Tips](https://www.anaheim.net/1442/Water-Energy-Saving-Tips)
  6. Washer & Dryer


#  Washer & Dryer 
  * Check to see that the outside moisture exhaust of the dryer is open and remove any lint that has collected.
  * Clean the lint filter before drying each load for quickest drying.
  * Don’t add wet items to a load that is partly dried.


![Save Energy ](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=9559)
  * Don’t open the dryer door unnecessarily. Warm air escapes from the dryer into the room.
  * Don’t over-wash or over-dry clothes.
  * Dry 2 or more loads in a row, taking advantage of the heat still in the dryer from the first load.
  * Dry full loads of clothes, but don’t overfill the dryer. One washer load is 1 dryer load. Clothes should tumble freely.
  * Presoak or use the soak cycle when washing heavily soiled items.
  * Purchase a high-efficiency clothes washer or dryer. Anaheim offers rebates for ENERGY STAR-rated clothes washers.
  * Run full loads or adjust the water level to fit the size of your load.
  * Separate lightweight from heavyweight fabrics for faster, more even drying.
  * Use the correct amount of detergent. Too much will make your machine work harder and use more energy.
  * Wash with warm or cold water rather than hot water when possible.
  * When drying only 1 or 2 items, add several compatible items to ensure proper tumbling and faster drying.

  5. [Save Water](https://www.anaheim.net/717/Save-Water)
  6. Water Supply 


#  Water Supply 
## California's Water Supply Outlook is Serious Business
Over the years, it has become increasingly evident that water use efficiency is essential for sustaining adequate and reliable water supplies for southern California. With the Colorado River experiencing historic, extended drought since 2000, the uncertainty of a long-term fix for the Sacramento-San Joaquin Bay Delta, and increased population growth, California is faced with long-term water supply reliability challenges.
In addition, the increasing costs of conveying and treating water supplies and replacing aging infrastructure are requiring water purveyors across the state to implement viable and cost-effective solutions to these issues. For a highly urbanized area such as Anaheim, water use efficiency continues to be the most cost-effective approach to ensuring reliable water supplies for its customers. Learn more about the comprehensive [](https://www.mwdh2o.com/)[conservation plan](https://www.mwdh2o.com/) to resolve the crisis in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta and provide a reliable water supply for California.
The Bay Delta Conservation Plan (BDCP) is a collaborative approach to restore the Sacramento-San Joaquin Bay Delta’s ecosystem and protect California’s water supplies. [](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LkEzTuxVCyA)[Watch a 10-minute video](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LkEzTuxVCyA) by the California Department of Water Resources.
In 2018 Governor Brown signed into law the water conservation bills SB 606 and AB 1668. These bills were a result of an Executive Order from the Governor during the recent drought which required the State agencies to develop and recommend a long-term water conservation framework—Making Water Conservation a California Way of Life-- to provide a road map to ensure adequate water supplies for now and in the future for the State.
##  As of July 18th, 2023, Anaheim has entered a Water Reduction Plan 1 which requires voluntary water saving measures.**However the State of California still requires the following restrictions as of May 24, 2023:**  
  

  * Avoid watering lawns in a manner that causes runoff or watering within 48 hours after measurable precipitation.
  * Avoid using a hose to wash a motor vehicle, except where the hose is fitted with a shutoff nozzle.
  * Do not wash down driveways and sidewalks with potable water, except to address an immediate health and safety need.
  * Avoid use of potable water in a fountain or decorative water feature, unless the water is re-circulated.
  * Promptly repair all water leaks from indoor and outdoor plumbing fixtures.


Making water conservation a California way of life ensures that our water demands are met in the future- regardless of the conditions of any one year.**Let’s do our part to protect our vital water resources.**

#  2015 State of the City 
Mayor Tom Tait   
2015 State of the City  
February 3, 2015  
[2015 State of the City Video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vFdI956ispg)  
  
Wow. Anaheim is on the move. There is no better city than Anaheim. Period.  
  
Think about it. We live in America – in Southern California – in Orange County. What’s better than that?  
  
We’re a world-class destination with Disneyland Resort celebrating its 60th anniversary,  
the largest Convention Center on the West Coast, and a Resort District delivering extraordinary experiences to over 20 million of visitors every year.  
  
We’re a major league sports city with two awesome teams – the Anaheim Ducks who currently hold one of the best records in hockey and just three weeks ago retired the number 8 worn by the Finnish Flash, the great Teemu Selanne, and Angels Baseball, coming off the best regular season in all of baseball, led by American League MVP Mike Trout!   
  
We have a vibrant downtown exemplified by the extraordinary Anaheim Packing District, with great restaurants, craft breweries and live music. Hundreds of Anaheim residents and visitors alike are flocking every night to this new gathering place in the heart of downtown.  
  
We have many exciting, distinct local neighborhoods, with a fantastic mix of ethnic diversity second to none in America, making us an incredibly interesting, authentic and hip place.  
  
We have a thriving arts community with our own classical orchestra, the Orange County Symphony now in its 30th year; our own ballet company now giving Anaheim global exposure with its groundbreaking online video series attracting over 50 million online hits on YouTube; the Art Crawl featuring the work of hundreds of local artists; a passionate Arts Council; and the award-winning, cutting-edge Chance Theatre.   
  
We have (at long last) the ARTIC – which connects our visitors and ourselves to all of Anaheim and everywhere else. It’s beautiful building generating a lot of buzz. You should check it out, especially at night.  
  
We have a well-run city that’s staffed with dedicated and creative people from all walks of life and led by our interim city manager, Paul Emery. And under the new direction of our police chief, Raul Quezada, crime is down 14% this past year.  
  
Anaheim is a great place to live for young and old alike. In our audience today we have longtime Anaheim resident, Elmer Thill who is soon going to turn 101-years-old. Julie and I had the honor of attending Elmer and Genevieve’s 75th wedding anniversary about a year and a half ago. Elmer – we salute you!  
  
And we have someone really special to my wife, Julie and me. He’s our one-year-old grandson, Hudson Eugene DeFrancis. Whitney, pick him up and show us your son!  
  
Right there in front of us, we have a 100-year life span -- both Anaheimers thriving in our city.   
  
And by the way, I’m never ever going to forget to introduce my best friend and wonderful wife – Hudson’s grandmother – Julie.  
  
And, we are the City of Kindness. We launched the Kindness initiative when I first took office, and it’s building momentum every year. Last year the elementary students at the Anaheim City School District committed to perform A Million Acts of Kindness. And they’re almost there. I’m told they’ll reach this milestone by early spring.  
  
Just recently, 4,000 of our junior high and high school kids in the Anaheim Union High School District volunteered for a Serve-a-thon on Martin Luther King Day – helping those in need and cleaning up neighborhoods throughout the city.  
  
Please help me thank our kids from all our school districts - the Anaheim City, Anaheim Union High School, Magnolia, Savanna, Centralia, Placentia-Yorba Linda, and Orange Unified – for lifting our city with their acts of kindness.  
  
So with all we have to offer, it’s no surprise that Anaheim is the best place to do business in our state. The Freedom-centered regulatory-relief culture we’ve developed the last four years makes it easier and less costly to do business here.  
  
Two years ago, I was pleased to have the Mayor’s Regulatory Relief Task Force recommend ways to streamline our processes and cut red tape. Since the Anaheim City Council approved these recommendations we have steadily increased the number of business licenses pulled each year.   
  
And the facts are in. In 2012 it was 4,000 new business licenses. In 2013, 4,200 new licenses were pulled. And last year, we saw a 13% increase with over 4,700 new business licenses.  
  
A significant percentage of these new licenses are being issued to immigrants who have come to live in Anaheim from all over the world.   
  
These new residents continually impress me. They bring remarkable energy and are driven to succeed in their new homeland.  
  
Not only are thousands of new businesses opening up--they’re building stuff: commercial, industrial and residential-All of it! Our Building Division is swamped with permit applications!   
  
In 2012, we served 14,800 customers and in 2013 it increased to almost 16 thousand. And last year the Building Division served 18,199 customers – a 14% increase over last year.   
  
Nationally, the increase in permit volume is 4.5%. In Anaheim, our 14% increase is three times the national average. Three times. Our construction industry is very strong and you all know that means our Anaheim economy is growing. This is particularly evident in the Platinum Triangle, where currently there are 1,438 new residential units under construction, with thousands more in the queue.   
  
Year after year -- due to good policy, hard work and a clear commitment to being a freedom centered city -- businesses of all kinds are choosing Anaheim.   
  
I promise you - we’ll never stop. Because a growing business community pays off in big ways – more manufacturing, more high-tech, more jobs and more great places to EAT and DRINK!  
  
Speaking of drinking, we’re now truly becoming THE Brew City of Southern California. By the close of this year, we’ll have 11 active craft breweries and more on the way. It helps when the planning staff approving the plans is cheering you on-like Scott Keohm, an associate planner and a craft beer aficionado.   
  
But that’s what Anaheim is all about – not just breweries, but all of our local small businesses -- our city staff is cheering you on as you fulfill your dreams.   
  
One of my jobs as Mayor is to run a financially disciplined city. I’m pleased to announce that for the fourth year in a row, our budget is balanced without drawing on reserves. Revenues continue to grow and we’re managing to keep our spending in line.  
  
While keeping our budget in balance, we’ve been steadfast in accomplishing our planned initiatives for 2015 concentrating on strengthening public safety, neighborhoods, infrastructure and economic development.   
  
For example, the City was recently awarded a grant of nearly $500,000 to reexamine plans for the Beach Boulevard corridor. The goal is to revitalize Beach Boulevard by attracting quality development while ensuring it compliments the well-maintained surrounding neighborhoods. In partnership with the community, we have developed a new plan and issued RFPs last month.  
  
Our message to developers and businesses is: There’s BIG opportunity in West Anaheim! Look what has happened in two short years downtown with the Packing District. This can happen in West Anaheim, too. The opportunity is there!  
  
In addition, the city council has recently instituted the Quality Rental Housing Program and updated regulations for short-term rental units to address quality of life issues in our neighborhoods.  
  
The City Council, City Manager and I are all committed to balancing our budget every year and not drawing on our reserves. This is critical to the continued health and well being of our city  
  
I’m also committed to creating a city hall that is accountable to every resident and business. This means having a city government that is transparent and open. When I first took office, I challenged our city staff to come up with ideas that would bring visibility to everything we’re doing. And we’re making progress.   
  
Today we’re launching the Open Data Portal – a website providing city facts, city budgets, statistics and reports to all interested parties.  
  
Just look at this: (see slide)   
From crime mapping to mining new business permit applications, we’re releasing this data to the public - to app developers, community organizations, businesses and individuals.   
  
For example - if you’re one of the developers thinking about investing in Beach Boulevard, right now you can go and find the kind of businesses already operating there, what new businesses are pulling licenses, and all sorts of things about West Anaheim.  
  
The Open Data Portal will create economic activity that we can’t even foresee today. And it makes us more responsive to the needs of all of our citizens. This is transparency and accountability at work for our city.  
  
In addition, this past November our citizens voted for two foundational changes that make the city government more responsive to them.  
  
The people approved a ballot initiative to both expand the city council and change how the council is elected. After a passionate debate, the citizens of Anaheim made a clear decision.  
  
After the November 2016 election, we will have 6 city council members in addition to the mayor. And each district will elect their own councilmember to represent them. In last November’s election, nearly 70% of the electorate voted to to create districts. The new district maps will be completed by the end of this year, so we can all prepare for the 2016 elections. This change is all about bringing city hall closer to the people we serve.  
  
As I begin my last four years as Mayor, I’m determined to leave Anaheim a much better, more vibrant and kinder city for all our residents and all of our businesses.  
  
We do have some big challenges though.   
  
I know we’re all concerned about the Angels’ issue.  
  
Like most of us, I’m a big fan of the Angels and I definitely do not want them to leave. However, my fiduciary duty is to the people of Anaheim - to be the steward of their largest real estate asset. I believe a deal can be found that’s good for the Angels and aligns with their plans, and that is fair and equitable to the people of Anaheim. Along with the city council, we will work to make this happen.  
  
There are two national challenges we as a city don’t have the jurisdiction to solve. But we live with the consequences every day. And these issues need to be addressed.   
  
The first is pension reform. This is an issue in almost every larger city in America and Anaheim is certainly not immune. Here in Anaheim, we have a public pension hole of more than 500 million dollars. To put this in perspective, our annual general fund budget is about half that.  
  
This matters for two significant reasons.  
  
First, money that goes into higher pension contributions is money we don’t have to serve our citizens. It’s the crowding out effect – crowding out our ability to build parks, roads, public safety and create a thriving business environment.  
  
Second, retirement benefits are guaranteed. If there’s not enough money then there’s ultimately only two ways to get it – raise taxes or cut services. This is the reality.  
  
You may not know this – I didn’t before I became Mayor. But the state of California doesn’t allow local governments to change the accrual formulas – even on a going-forward basis. I’ve been actively advocating for these changes and will continue to do so in order to protect our employees’ retirement and make the pensions sustainable. In the meantime, we need to do everything we can do to establish the lowest possible pension rates that the state currently allows for new hires.  
  
The other national challenge I want to address is the broken immigration system. We don’t have the authority to fix it, but as I said, we have to live with the consequences.  
  
As a city council, we unanimously voted for a resolution asking Congress to pass comprehensive immigration reform. I can tell you cities all over America are doing the same.  
  
Along with securing our borders and streamlining the visa process, there’s an important moral dimension at play here. It’s vital for any immigration legislation to enable people to come out of the shadows and fully pursue the American Dream for themselves and their families.  
  
We may disagree on the wisdom of Presidential executive actions. But we can all agree the current system is broken, and a law to fix this system needs to be passed by the Congress and signed by the President.   
  
I am working right now to help make this happen with the Mayors of America.   
  
Which brings me to something I talked about two years ago from this podium.   
  
Regardless of issues surrounding services, income and education discrepancies, we are all Anaheim.  
  
This isn’t an aspirational sentiment. It’s a simple fact. We live, work and grow together. We are Anaheim.  
  
Our citizens and businesses have been working for years to strengthen the bond between us - with programs like Hi Neighbor, Coming Home Anaheim and great civic events like the annual Halloween Parade.  
  
Did you know that the city program, Coming Home Anaheim, has placed 265 of our homeless citizens in good housing this past year? It’s estimated that the net societal dollar cost savings of moving these individuals from the street is over $2.2 million dollars. However, it’s not the savings in dollars that motivates our community to get involved; it’s the savings in human lives that inspires us.  
  
This partnership of over 87 community-based non-profits and faith-based groups, along with our own city police force and parks staff, have made tremendous strides. But we’re far from finished. We will tackle this issue with determination, compassion and kindness.  
  
One of the things I plan to work on specifically this year is housing our homeless veterans. Let’s set a goal that there will be no veterans living without shelter in our city by this time next year. Can I get your commitment to this goal right now?  
  
In spite of the good things happening in our city, too many of our Anaheim youth are at risk. Too many are driven to drugs, gangs and petty crime. Too many are simply not reaching their potential. We must pull together as one to overcome it.   
  
In 2012, Disneyland Resort funded a youth services assessment study, conducted by the Olin Group. The report specifically explored services offered to our at-risk youth. Here’s what they found. We have a youth-centric city, with a higher percentage of youth than most other Orange County cities.  
  
We have the 2nd highest teen pregnancy rates in Orange County. In the Anaheim City School District 85% of our kids are on a free or reduced lunch program. Thousands of our students are homeless or living in cars, motels or with other families sleeping on the floor.  
  
Gangs are a growing threat to our children. We have to defend against them. Our police officers tell us children as young as 9 years of age are vulnerable to being recruited into gang membership.  
  
These are the same kids who are delivering on their promise of A Million Acts of Kindness. They are wonderful children – but they are truly AT-RISK!   
  
There have been two responses to this study:  
  
First, the City of Anaheim, as an organization, has responded by bringing programs directly to where the kids live.  
  
Led by our Community Services department, we’ve expanded program capacity and facilities serving families and kids in the city – programs like “Mobile Recreation” and “Higher Ground” in Lincoln Park.  
  
And they’ve focused on making programs for teens more relevant to their lives – teen pregnancy reduction programs, opening two new Support Anaheim Youth teen centers. We call this Project SAY. This program targets at-risk youth, teaching character and life skills.  
  
We are also focused on reducing crime and making all neighborhoods safe. We’ve increased the number of sworn police officers, empowered our community policing teams, become the first in the County to purchase body cameras, and added more streetlights all over the city.  
  
And most important, the police force is working daily to build trust between the police and the whole community. Outstanding programs like Cops for Kids, the Police Explorers and GRIP are breaking down the false barriers between the police and our kids.   
At Sycamore Junior High, the Public Safety Career Pipeline was launched. Our police department, along with Anaheim Fire & Rescue provided weekly programming to 8th grade students – turning them on to careers in public safety and we plan to expand the program to other schools as well  
  
And the Public Safety Board is up and running, strengthening confidence and accountability between the police and the community.   
  
Please help me thank our incredible and trusted police force. They have a tough job and they put their lives on the line for us everyday.  
  
Everything I’ve said so far has been about the city’s response to our youth at risk.   
  
The second response has come from the business community, led by the Disneyland Resort, the Anaheim Ducks and Angels Baseball. These three giants of our community have stepped up to the challenge.  
  
Last year, I told you about Accelerate Change Together for Anaheim – ACT Anaheim for short. A lot has happened since then. Take a look.   
  
(Play ACT video.)  
  
I want to personally thank Michael Colglazier and the Disneyland Resort, Henry and Susan Samueli, Arte and Carole Moreno, and the Orange County Community Foundation, and their President Shelley Hoss for making all this possible. Thank you.  
  
This is a simple, but revolutionary idea. Led by Disneyland Resort, the Ducks and the Angels, the business community has created a collective fund that will send resources to our non-profit programs and agencies that are successfully bringing opportunities to our children throughout the city.  
  
Every business needs to be part of this.   
  
Last October, something happened that shook our city. Ximena Meza was an exemplary 4th grader at Juliette Low Elementary. She loved playing soccer, learning karate and dancing Zumba in the park. She was known for being helpful to everyone she came across. On October 22 Ximena found herself in the cross fire of two rival gangs. This happy 9-year old girl was killed in front of her apartment while playing with her sister and friend, a tragic victim of gang violence.   
  
This happened in our city…our city of kindness. What happened here is not acceptable. Everyone who lives here, everyone who works here, has to do more. We need to get to the root causes of gang violence, and address those causes. We must face these issues more effectively than in the past, and all of us need to get involved in some way.   
  
What can or should we do? I believe our job is to show these at-risk kids that there are positive options for them. They can find fulfillment and happiness doing the right things. It’s within their reach.  
  
We simply can’t sit on our hands. We can’t drive by these issues like we drive by these neighborhoods on the freeway.  
  
If we do nothing different, then it’s logical to conclude that what happened to Ximena will happen to another child. Therefore, we must act. And act now. Kindness is an ACTION word. We cannot be kind and do nothing.  
  
So, what should we do? We need the business community’s deep, long-lasting and deliberate involvement. We need your time, talent and treasure.  
  
Let me tell you about something that happened a little more than a year ago. 200 Anaheim high school students came to city hall with a petition signed by 5000 students asking for help. They were asking for help in preparing for a 21st century career. They wanted help preparing to compete in a global economy. Specifically, they are asking for help from you.  
  
We all know what the power of a mentor can do to help a young person. A simple act of kindness has the potential to turn a life around or give it direction. Knowing this, we initiated the Mayor’s Mentoring program, in partnership with the Anaheim Union High School District.   
  
Kaiser Permanente was among the first to respond. Just two weeks ago, I went to Kaiser’s Anaheim facility and saw “time and talent” in action. Take a look.  
  
(Video plays)  
  
Amazing. I left Kaiser that day optimistic and at the same time, with a deepened sense of urgency. There are so many more students who need to see the kind of careers they can have if they work hard. And they need to know we care about them and will help them. We need to reach them ALL.  
  
I’ve heard from many businesses already: Toyota Financial Services, Bunzl Distributing, Disneyland Resort, Wells Fargo, and many others.   
  
Join us. The benefits are real – of course the individual students will benefit in immeasurable ways. But employees and businesses will benefit as well. There are numerous studies that show businesses actually improve their bottom line by getting involved in the community. In other words, doing good is good for business.  
  
If mentoring doesn’t work for you, then there are other ways to help. If your time and talent aren’t available, then consider sharing a bit of your Treasure.  
  
We’re asking you to contribute to ACT Anaheim today. I can do this with confidence because I know that ACT Anaheim is a solid investment. Your dollars will be thoughtfully managed and leveraged with those of other partners -- partners who share the same vision and goals for the future of Anaheim.   
  
We’ve started a list. If you’re not on it, you should be. Everyone doing business in Anaheim needs to be on this list, as a contributor to ACT Anaheim.  
  
I’ve never been more serious in my life. We have a generation in need. And it’s going to take a generation of dedication from all of us to meet this need.   
  
Our task is to reach all of our youth. And ACT Anaheim is doing that – with a laser focus on the needs identified in the Youth Services Assessment.  
  
We’ve looked deeply into our community. We understand what it needs to be successful. It’s time to bring business partners together not just from Anaheim but also from all over Orange County to meet these needs.  
  
Time, Talent, Treasure. Just imagine what we’ll do together – Our Anaheim deliberately and effectively bringing real opportunities to every one of our children.  
  
Imagine our great city free of gangs, drugs and fear. Imagine thousands of mentors in our schools, a police force trusted in every neighborhood, fully funded charities and community programs doing their great work.  
  
Imagine a city where what happened to Ximena Never-ever happens again…where no Anaheim family ever again has to experience the loss of a child through gang violence.  
  
Imagine a city where all of our children are nurtured spiritually, intellectually and physically. And imagine a city where every child in every neighborhood sees they can create lives that make them happy, hopeful and serve the greater good.   
  
Here’s a great example of what works.  
  
Let me tell you about Nancy Sanchez. She grew up in Anaheim. She watched as many of her peers made bad choices because they thought that was all they could do. Some of them turned to gangs for a sense of belonging. And some of their lives were ruined before they grew up.  
  
As a little girl, Nancy was exposed to music, specifically by RHYTHMO Mariachi, an Anaheim musical charity, and she found her authentic voice. Soon she was singing Latin jazz and then, modern jazz and pop music. Today, she’s a successful singer and songwriter with two albums to her credit. You can hear her singing in venues throughout Southern California. In fact, you might have heard her perform at the NAMM show two weekends ago. And you’re about to hear her now….ladies and gentlemen, please welcome one of our own, Nancy Sanchez.  
  
Nancy to sing one song, “It’s a Wonderful World”, with her band.  
  
Isn’t she something? Nancy is a perfect example of someone who found a mentor and a program that opened her up to see her potential.  
  
We need to get to every young person in our city. We have to get to them now so they choose a life of fulfillment and success.  
  
I can’t force you to join us. That’s not how kindness works. However, the need is real. And time is of the essence.  
  
I can tell you that both the Mentoring Program and ACT Anaheim are up and running - and they both work.  
  
So here’s the big ask: Please get involved!   
  
Take the card at your table, fill it out and we will come to you. Or go back to your offices and talk to your teams and get back to us.   
  
I believe you will all want to get involved. I believe you want to pull together as One Anaheim and change the lives of an entire generation of our children. Well, now is the time.  
  
This can be your legacy-our legacy- to the youth of Anaheim. I know when you’re old and at the end of your life, you’ll look back with pride at what was accomplished in this city-our city.  
  
I’m so proud of our city. I’m so excited to see what we can do together. We are all Anaheim. God bless you and God bless our great city. Thank you.  
  
Phillip Palmer returns to the stage and announces $70,000 in net proceeds was raised from today’s State of the City event to support ACT Anaheim.

#  Military Banner Program 
The City of Anaheim is proud to recognize the brave men and women who are serving in the Armed Forces through the Military Banner Program. The Military Banner Program is designed to honor Anaheim residents that are currently deployed in our nations’ military and we have honored 136 service members since the inception in 2010.  

We recognize their service and commitment to our nation and our community through recognition on this webpage, as well as with a [banner](https://www.anaheim.net/DocumentCenter/View/3873) hung in various locations throughout the City of Anaheim to show our public expression of gratitude.
To submit applications please visit our [Application Form](http://www.anaheim.net/1700/Application-Form).
For questions regarding the Military Banner Program, please call 714-765-5162 or send an email.
  

  1. [Application Form](https://www.anaheim.net/1700/Application-Form)

  6. Veterans Tribute


#  Veterans Tribute 
The city of Anaheim is proud to recognize the brave men and women of the armed forces through active service recognition and honoring of Anaheim veterans.
  
Anaheim Veterans Day Celebration
Veterans from all branches are honored in Anaheim on a special Veterans Day celebration hosted by the city of Anaheim. The ceremony is held at the city's Veterans Monument in downtown Anaheim. The public is invited to join us nd pay tribute to the brave men and women who willingly served our country.
For more information on city events please visit our [events calendar](https://www.anaheim.net/Calendar.aspx).
  1. ![Veterans sitting down looking forward](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c20%5c404%2fAnaheim+Veterans+Day+Celebration_201502200815138531.jpg)
  2. ![People walking trough the street showing their support by wearing red](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c20%5c404%2fAnaheim+Community+Events_201502200815137124.jpg)
  3. ![Veterans sitting down one with his thumb up](https://www.anaheim.net/1626/Veterans-Tribute)
  4. ![Veteran standing by a red and blue wreath](https://www.anaheim.net/1626/Veterans-Tribute)
  5. ![13th Marine Expeditionary Unit standing behind their banner](https://www.anaheim.net/1626/Veterans-Tribute)
  6. ![Four fallen hero banners on street poles for display](https://www.anaheim.net/1626/Veterans-Tribute)
  7. ![Uniformed soldiers standing by a banner of a fallen hero](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Path?filePath=%2fdocuments%5cIntranet%5c20%5c404%2fHonor+Fallen+Heroes_201502200815143062.jpg)


## Anaheim Community Events
  

Each year, Anaheim honors our veterans and active military personnel at special events including:
  * Memorial Day
  * Flag Day parade
  * Fourth of July parade
  * Veterans Day celebration
  * Military banner ceremony
  * Yellow ribbon experience


For more on city events please visit our [events calendar](https://www.anaheim.net/Calendar.aspx).
  

## Anaheim Veterans Gallery
  

The Anaheim Veterans Gallery honors veterans who submit a personal photo and a brief story of their service to our country. To be part of the Anaheim Veterans Gallery please fill out our online [Veterans Gallery application form](https://www.anaheim.net/FormCenter/City-Administration-10/Veterans-Gallery-Application-Form-75).
  

## Anaheim Veterans Monument
  

The Anaheim Veterans Monument is next to the Muzeo in downtown Anaheim at 241 S. Anaheim Blvd. Anaheim's 9-foot high Veterans Monument was dedicated in 1999 and designed by Anaheim artist Richard Turner. The monument, made from bronze and concrete, depicts all branches of the military from wars of the 20th century. Turner's research in the Anaheim Library led him to include images depicting actual Anaheim residents. The monument is flanked by fountains, representative of water washing away years of conflict.
## Military Banner Program
  

The [Military Banner Program](https://www.anaheim.net/1681/Military-Banner-Program) is designed to honor Anaheim residents that are currently deployed in our military. Active military personnel are honored and recognized through a city dedicated webpage, and with a banner displayed on a streetlight pole in various locations throughout Anaheim to show the city's public expression of gratitude. The city has proudly recognized 72 honorees since the inception of the program.
  

## Honor Fallen Heroes
  

The city honors Anaheim residents who served in the military that gave the ultimate sacrifice to protect our country. In respect for our [fallen heroes](https://www.anaheim.net/1703/Fallen-Heroes), Anaheim pays special tribute at the Military Banner Ceremony and honors them with a military banner in each of three designated banner locations. In addition, the city honors these fallen heroes by recognizing them on a dedicated City webpage.
  

## Purple Heart City
  

February 2014, the Anaheim City Council proclaimed Anaheim a Purple Heart City. The Purple Heart is given to military members who are wounded or killed in combat. Cities are eligible for recognition under the national Military Order of the Purple Heart. California became the nation’s first Purple Heart State in 2012, and Orange County is poised to become a Purple Heart County in 2013.

#  Government Relations 
As a division of the City Manager's Office, the Government Relations Division operates as the City's primary liaison with federal, state and local government entities. Through coalition building, strategic planning and relationship management, the Government Relations team actively promotes the City's projects and priorities while working to shape legislation that affects the City.  
  
Responsibilities  
Government Relations is responsible for providing policy support for the Mayor, Council and City Manager's Office, supplying research, analysis and policy recommendations when necessary. 
### Contact Us
  1. ## Alyssa Guerrero
Staff analyst
200 S. Anaheim Blvd.  
Suite 773  
Anaheim, CA 92805
(714) 765-4610

#  Get Involved 
13th Marine Expeditionary Unit (13th MEU)  
The 13th Marine Expeditionary Unit, stationed out of Camp Pendleton was formally adopted by the Anaheim City Council in 2007 to show support for the military personnel and their families.  
  
The 13th MEU adoption committee works effortlessly to support the 13th MEU Marines and Sailors and their families on an ongoing basis, both when the unit is at home and deployed. In return, the USMC will provide community support by participating in local school programs and various city functions. 
![A group of 4 Marines representing the 13th MEU](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentID=3922)
To learn more about the [13th MEU adoption committee](https://www.anaheim.net/1673/Anaheim-Marine-Adoption-Committee) and how to support Anaheim's adopted military unit, please visit our [Donations and Sponsors page](https://www.anaheim.net/FormCenter/City-Administration-10/Military-Banner-Sponsors-78).

#  Command Staff 
## **Chief Rick Armendariz**
![Chief Rick Armendariz](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64472)
Chief Rick Armendariz serves as the 37th Chief of Police for the Anaheim Police Department, leading the largest city law enforcement agency in Orange County. He oversees a team of approximately 600 employees, including over 400 sworn officers, and is committed to fostering strong relationships between the Department and the community it serves.
Chief Armendariz actively collaborates with the Chief’s Advisory Committee, the Chief’s Neighborhood Advisory Council, and the Chief’s Youth Advisory Council to address public safety concerns and improve quality of life throughout Anaheim. He also serves on several boards and organizations, including the Board of the Boys and Girls Club, the Bridges at Kraemer Place Community Advisory Board, and holds a Regional Chair position with California’s Police Officers Memorial. Additionally, he is a member of the California Peace Officers' Association, the YMCA, Rotary Club, and the Orange County Chiefs of Police and Sheriffs Association.
Appointed Chief in August 2023, Chief Armendariz previously served as the Department’s Deputy Chief for over four years. Prior to his tenure in Anaheim, he was the Assistant Chief of Police for the Modesto Police Department from 2015 to 2019.
Chief Armendariz began his law enforcement career with the Modesto Police Department at age 23. Rising through the ranks from Police Officer to Assistant Chief, he served in a variety of specialized units and leadership roles, including patrol, gang enforcement, crimes against children, violent crimes, internal affairs, and public/media relations. He also commanded the Crisis Response Unit (SWAT), the K9 Unit, and an FBI Safe Streets Task Force.
In addition to his law enforcement duties, Chief Armendariz served as Modesto’s Deputy City Manager for two years, leading the city’s Safe Neighborhood Initiative. During his time as Captain and Assistant Chief, he played a key role in transforming the Modesto Police Department into a more community-engaged and intelligence-led organization. His efforts included re-establishing Area Commands, launching a Real-Time Crime Center, implementing predictive policing technologies, and expanding crime analysis and youth engagement programs.
Currently, as Anaheim’s Police Chief, he is spearheading several initiatives, including the development of a Real-Time Crime Center, the expansion of Community Policing strategies, the creation of a Tourism-Oriented Police Team, and the launch of a Drones as First Responders Unit. He has also led the revitalization of youth outreach efforts, such as expanding the School Resource Officer program and introducing new youth-focused programs. Chief Armendariz supported the growth of the Department’s Peer Support Program, "Women of Anaheim," which focuses on empowerment, connection, and healing. His leadership was instrumental in navigating the Department through the challenges of the COVID-19 pandemic and returning to normal operations.
Chief Armendariz is a graduate of the FBI National Academy in Quantico, Virginia, and Boston University’s Senior Management Institute for Police. He holds a Master of Science in Emergency Services Administration from California State University, Long Beach, and a Bachelor of Arts in Criminal Justice from Chapman University.
## Deputy Chief Charles “Chuck” Cinnamo
![Deputy Chief Charles Chuck Cinnamo](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64467)Deputy Chief Charles “Chuck” Cinnamo serves as the Deputy Chief of Police for the Anaheim Police Department, bringing nearly three decades of law enforcement leadership and a strong foundation in public safety operations to the role.
A native of Southern California, Deputy Chief Cinnamo holds a Bachelor of Arts degree in Vocational Education from San Diego State University. He also earned two Associate of Science degrees from Cuyamaca College in Computer Technology and Electronics Technology.
Before entering law enforcement, Deputy Chief Cinnamo worked for the San Diego Unified School District as an Instructional Aide, teaching math to special education students. This experience shaped his approach to mentorship and leadership.
He began his law enforcement career in 1996 with the San Diego County Sheriff’s Department, graduating from the Corrections Academy. Later that same year, he attended the San Diego Regional Public Safety Training Institute at Miramar College to become a Deputy Sheriff. Over his tenure with the Sheriff's Department, he served in various assignments and rose through the ranks from Corrections Deputy to Assistant Sheriff.
His early assignments included working in corrections at the Central Detention Facility and San Diego Central Jail, including jail administration and training. As a Deputy Sheriff, he served in patrol in communities such as Lemon Grove, Spring Valley, Imperial Beach, and Bonita, and later transitioned into investigative roles, including service as a School Resource Officer and area investigator.
As a Sergeant and Lieutenant, he held supervisory and management assignments in patrol, traffic, investigations, community-oriented policing, and weapons training. Upon promotion to Captain, he led operations at the Communication Center, oversaw Property and Evidence, and served as “Chief of Police” for the Vista Station. He then commanded the department’s Narcotics and Gangs Division.
As Commander and later Assistant Sheriff, Deputy Chief Cinnamo provided executive oversight to major functional areas of the Sheriff’s Department, including Centralized Investigations, the Regional Crime Lab, Jail Medical Services, Jail Investigations, and the Sheriff’s Transportation Detail.
In 2024, he joined the Anaheim Police Department as Deputy Chief of Police, continuing his commitment to professional excellence, collaboration, and innovation in public safety.
Deputy Chief Cinnamo is a graduate of several premier law enforcement leadership programs, including the FBI National Academy (Session 253), the Police Executive Research Forum’s Senior Management Institute for Police, and California POST’s Supervisor School, Management Course, and Executive Development Course. He also graduated from the Drug Enforcement Administration’s Drug Unit Commanders Academy.
He is an active member of the International Association of Chiefs of Police (IACP), the Police Executive Research Forum (PERF), the California Police Chiefs Association (CPCA), the California Peace Officers’ Association (CPOA), and the Retired Deputy Sheriff’s Association of San Diego County.
## Captain Rodney Duckwitz
![Captain Rodney Duckwitz](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64471)Captain Rodney Duckwitz was born and raised in Anaheim and is a graduate of Loara High School, Class of 1987. He began his service with the City of Anaheim in 1993 as a Code Enforcement Officer before joining the Anaheim Police Department the following year. He graduated from the Fullerton College Police Academy in 1993.
As a police officer, Captain Duckwitz served in a variety of assignments, including Patrol, Traffic, Community Policing, and the Mounted Enforcement Unit. He was promoted to sergeant in 2015, where he continued to lead in Patrol and Traffic Safety while also supervising the Mounted Enforcement Unit.
Upon his promotion to lieutenant in 2019, Captain Duckwitz took on a broad range of leadership roles. His assignments included Patrol Watch Commander, the Orange County Family Justice Center, and the Investigations Division. While in Investigations, he oversaw major sections including Homicide, Robbery and Major Assaults, Gangs, VICE, Family Crimes, Sexual Assault, Criminal Intelligence, and Forensics.
Promoted to the rank of captain in 2023, Captain Duckwitz has served as the Commanding Officer of both the Support Services Division and the Investigations Division. He currently serves as the Commanding Officer of the Operations Division, overseeing Personnel and Training, Traffic Safety, Strategic Planning, Emergency Management, the Real-Time Crime Center, and Crime Analysis. He also manages the Department’s wellness and support programs, including the Peer Support Team and Chaplain Program. In addition, he holds ancillary responsibility as the Commander of the Mounted Enforcement Unit.
Captain Duckwitz is a graduate of the POST Executive Development Course and the Police Executive Research Forum’s Senior Management Institute for Police (Session 93). He holds an Associate of Arts degree in Business from Golden West College, a Bachelor of Science in Occupational Studies from California State University, Long Beach, a Master of Arts from Chapman University, and a Doctor of Education from the University of Southern California. His doctoral dissertation focused on housing and resource challenges faced by Commercially Sexually Exploited Children (CSEC) and other minors who are victims of human trafficking.
He is a member of the International Association of Chiefs of Police (IACP), the California Police Chiefs Association (Cal Chiefs), the Peace Officers Research Association of California (PORAC), the California Peace Officers’ Association (CPOA), and the Police Executive Research Forum (PERF).
##  ![Captain Curtis Faulkner](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64470)Captain Curtis Faulkner
Captain Curtis Faulkner was raised in Anaheim and is a proud graduate of Western High School, Class of 1988. He went on to earn a Bachelor of Science degree in Political Science from California State University, Fullerton, in 1994.
In 1995, Captain Faulkner began his law enforcement career with the Los Angeles Police Department. He graduated from the LAPD Academy in 1996 and served the department until 2001. During his time with LAPD, he held a variety of assignments as a police officer, including Patrol, Community Policing, Traffic, and as a Public Information Officer. As a detective, he worked in the Financial Crimes Unit and the Detective Training Unit.
Captain Faulkner joined the Anaheim Police Department in 2001, where he has served in numerous assignments throughout his career. As an officer, he worked in Patrol, Economic Crimes, and on the Primary Response Officer (PRO) Team. He was promoted to sergeant and went on to supervise Patrol, Personnel, and the PRO Team.
Upon promotion to lieutenant, Captain Faulkner held a wide range of leadership roles, including watch commander, supervisor of the PRO Team, and assignments in Personnel and Training, Internal Affairs, and as District Commander in the Central, South, and East Districts. He also oversaw investigations in the Crimes Against Persons Unit and served in the Special Operations Division.
As Captain of the Patrol Division, Captain Faulkner has been responsible for overseeing Patrol Operations, the K9 Unit, the Anaheim Detention Facility, and Air Support.
Captain Faulkner is a graduate of the Sherman Block Supervisory Leadership Institute (Class 298), and the Senior Management Institute for Police through the Police Executive Research Forum (Session 92). He maintains professional affiliations with the International Association of Chiefs of Police (IACP), the California Police Chiefs Association (Cal Chiefs), the Peace Officers Research Association of California (PORAC), the California Peace Officers’ Association (CPOA), and PERF.
## Captain Lorenzo Glenn
![Lorenzo Glenn](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64469)Captain Lorenzo Glenn began his law enforcement career in 1991 after graduating from Golden West College Police Academy (Class 103). He initially served with the Westminster Police Department, working in patrol until joining the Anaheim Police Department in December 1993.
As a police officer, Captain Glenn held a variety of assignments including Patrol, Gang Enforcement, Street Narcotics, Traffic, and Auto Theft investigations. He was promoted to sergeant in 2003 and went on to serve in Patrol, the Tourist Oriented Policing Detail (TOP), Internal Affairs, Administrative Services, Community Services, and K-9. He also launched the Department’s Peer Support Team in 2012, reflecting his long-standing commitment to officer wellness and resiliency.
In 2015, Captain Glenn was promoted to lieutenant and served as a Watch Commander and Traffic Section Commander. He later held roles as District Commander for the East and Central Districts, and subsequently, the West District, where he also oversaw the Homeless Outreach Team (HOT) and the Psychiatric Emergency Response Team (PERT). He later managed the Anaheim Detention Facility and Air Support Unit.
Captain Glenn currently serves as the Commanding Officer of the Operations Support Division, which includes the Department’s District Policing Teams, Community Engagement, Traffic, Youth Services, and Homeless and Mental Health Outreach.
A lifelong learner, Captain Glenn holds a Bachelor’s Degree in Sociology from the University of Montana, where he played college football as a student-athlete. He earned a Master’s Degree in Emergency Services Administration from California State University, Long Beach in 2008, and most recently, a Master’s Degree in Psychology with an emphasis in Human Factors from Grand Canyon University in 2024.
Captain Glenn is a graduate of the Sherman Block Supervisory Leadership Institute (Class of 2007), the California POST Command College (Class 65), and the FBI National Academy (Session 291). He is a former President of the Public Safety Peer Support Association (2016–2021) and is an active member of several professional organizations, including the International Association of Chiefs of Police (IACP), the California Police Chiefs Association (Cal Chiefs), the Police Executive Research Forum (PERF), and the National Organization of Black Law Enforcement Executives (NOBLE).
## Captain Eric Trapp
![Captain Eric Trapp](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64612)Captain Eric Trapp began his law enforcement career as a Police Explorer with the Newport Beach Police Department while in high school. In 1995, he was hired by the Anaheim Police Department and graduated from Class 124 of the Orange County Sheriff's Academy.
As a police officer, Captain Trapp served in a variety of assignments, including Primary Response Officer, Field Training Officer, and as part of the Resort Policing Detail. He was later promoted to detective and was assigned to the Sexual Assault/Family Crimes Detail. He was also a member of the Department’s Special Weapons and Tactics (SWAT) Team and served as an instructor for the Orange County Sheriff's Department Advanced Officer Training Program. In addition, he contributed to multiple advisory committees, including the North Orange County Regional Occupational Program.
Upon promotion to sergeant, Captain Trapp held several leadership assignments, including as the detective sergeant in the Family Crimes Detail, investigator in Internal Affairs, and the Department’s Public Information Officer. He also served as the Executive Officer for the Patrol Division and developed and managed the Department’s Critical Infrastructure Protection Program.
As a lieutenant, Captain Trapp served in a broad range of assignments, including Patrol Watch Commander, District Commander, Gang Suppression and Intervention Commander, Public Information Officer, Property Crimes Section Commander, Personnel and Training Commander, Youth Services Section Commander, and Resort Commander. He also led the Department’s Tactical Response Group, overseeing SWAT, Snipers, Tactical Negotiators, Mounted Enforcement, and the Mobile Field Force.
As a captain, he has commanded the Operations Support Division, overseeing District Policing Teams, Mental Health and Homeless Outreach initiatives, and both the Traffic and Youth Services Sections. He currently serves as the Commanding Officer of the Investigations Division, managing all investigative and undercover units within the Department, along with the Forensic Services Detail. He continues to lead the Department’s Tactical Response Group.
Captain Trapp holds a Bachelor of Science in Criminal Justice from California State University, Long Beach, and a Master of Science in Leadership and Management from the University of La Verne. He is a graduate of Session 283 of the FBI National Academy, the Police Executive Research Forum’s Senior Management in Policing program, and has completed executive education coursework at the Harvard Kennedy School of Government.
Captain Trapp and his wife, who is also a sergeant with the Anaheim Police Department, reside in the City of Anaheim.
## Police Support Services Administrator Kerrstyn Vega
![Kerrstyn Vega](https://www.anaheim.net/ImageRepository/Document?documentId=64468)Kerrstyn Vega began her public service career with the Anaheim Police Department in 2006 as a Records Specialist in the Records Bureau. Prior to joining the Department, she managed a retail photography studio, where she gained valuable experience in operations, customer service, and team leadership.
Over the years, Kerrstyn has advanced through a range of civilian leadership roles across multiple areas of the Department. Her assignments have included serving as a Senior Office Specialist and Staff Analyst in the Homeland Security and Emergency Management Bureau, where she supported critical preparedness and response initiatives. She later transitioned to the Budget and Finance Division as a Staff Analyst and Administrative Analyst, where she was eventually promoted to Administrative Services Manager.
In her current role as Police Support Services Manager, Kerrstyn oversees a wide portfolio of operational support functions, including the Budget and Finance Bureau (with oversight of the Payroll Unit), the Records Section (including Property & Evidence), the Communications Section, the Department’s Information Technology operations, and fleet and facility management.
Kerrstyn holds a Bachelor of Science degree in Criminal Justice Administration from California State University Dominguez Hills and a Master of Science degree in Homeland Security from San Diego State University.
She has completed several notable leadership programs, including the POST Civilian Management Course, the FBI-LEEDA Command Leadership Course, the City of Anaheim Profession of Management Course, the Harvard University Performance Leadership Course, and the Los Angeles County Sheriff’s Department Leadership Development Course. She is also a member of the National Homeland Security Association.
